cataloging_subject.label: Poema Morale (McClean 123 version, with opening couplet from Sinners Beware)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the physically continuous 337-line selection that Anna C. Paues transcribed from Fitzwilliam Museum MS McClean 123, fols. 115r–120r, and that CME00070 encodes from her 1907 edition. Paues identifies the first two copied lines as the opening of the separate work Sinners Beware; the following 335 lines are the McClean version of Poema Morale. The record therefore treats the encoded unit as a composite rather than silently assigning all 337 lines to Poema Morale. It excludes the rest of McClean 123, Paues's introductory study and apparatus as modern authorship, Trinity College MS B.14.52 as a comparison witness, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML artifact as distinct physical, editorial, and digital layers.

catalog_summary.title: Poema Morale (McClean 123 version, with opening couplet from Sinners Beware)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Poema Morale: late twelfth century; McClean composite copy: late thirteenth century or c. 1300
catalog_summary.region: England; Poema Morale tentatively localized to the southern South-East Midlands around Middlesex or London

titles.value: Poema Morale (McClean 123 version, with opening couplet from Sinners Beware)

assertion.notes: The qualifier is supplied to describe the actual composite electronic unit; it is not a historical manuscript title.

titles.value: A Newly Discovered Manuscript of the Poema Morale

scope.description: Title inherited by the U-M item from Paues's 1907 article

notes: This is the conventional scholarly title, not an authorial or manuscript title.

assertion.notes: Betty Hill proposed this descriptive title in 1977; Poema Morale remains the conventional identifier used by Paues and DIMEV.

notes: Historical English title reported in Hill's discussion of the work's names.

titles.value: Ich am elder þane ich was of wintre & of lore

notes: The selected two lines are the opening of the separately indexed 59-stanza work; Paues's title is retained for the fragment.

titles.value: Þe holi gostes miȝte, us alle helpe & diȝte, us wissie & us teche

scope.description: Direct transcription of the physically continuous text at McClean 123, fols. 115r–120r

notes: Hill reports that Paues transcribed the manuscript in February 1905.

scope.description: Textual comparison of the McClean version with Morris's printed T text and other published Poema Morale copies; direct consultation of manuscripts other than McClean 123 is not established

date_statements.display: Late twelfth century; Hill proposed 1154–1189, while an earlier metrical argument allowed approximately 1170–1200

assertion.notes: This dates the abstract work, not McClean 123. Hill reports earlier metrical reasoning that the original could not predate approximately 1170–1200 and gives her own view that it was first written during 1154–1189.

date_statements.display: Late thirteenth century or around 1300 in Fitzwilliam Museum MS McClean 123

assertion.notes: This dates the surviving copy in which the two-line Sinners Beware opening and the 335-line Poema Morale version are physically continuous; it does not date either abstract work's composition.

date_statements.display: First published by Anna C. Paues in Anglia 30 (1907), pp. 227–237

assertion.notes: This is the first publication of the newly discovered McClean version and its attached opening couplet, not the first publication of Poema Morale from any witness.

places.label: Southern South-East Midlands (Middlesex or London area)
notes: Hill's reconstruction concerns the original Poema Morale, not the dialect or production place of McClean 123.
Both represented components are Middle English religious verse. This broad cultural-origin statement does not identify a precise composition site.

assertion.notes: Hill reconstructed the original version on the southern border of the South-East Midland dialect area around Middlesex or London. This is a critical localization, not documentary proof.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The language of the McClean copy is a witness-level realization. Conflicting Kentish/Western, Essex, and central Gloucestershire localizations are not promoted here to the abstract work.

form_statements.description: Both components are verse. CME00070 contains one undivided LG with 337 L elements: a two-line opening fragment from Sinners Beware followed by 335 Poema Morale lines; DIMEV describes the abstract Poema Morale as 398 lines in rhyming couplets across its fullest witnesses.

assertion.notes: Hill explicitly calls the work a verse-sermon; verse homily is the normalized corpus label.

subject_statements.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Almsgiving and care for the poor

summaries.value: After a two-line invocation borrowed from or shared with the opening of Sinners Beware, the McClean Poema Morale speaker reflects on age, wasted life, repentance, charity, transient wealth, death, judgment, hell, heaven, and the need to live well before mercy is sought too late. The homiletic argument contrasts temporary earthly pleasure and possessions with eternal punishment or salvation and closes with a prayer for a good life and end.

Part 1 is a physically attached two-line opening from Sinners Beware. Part 2 is the 335-line McClean version of Poema Morale, one of seven complete witnesses indexed by DIMEV; the abstract work reaches 398 lines in its fullest copies.

CME00070 encodes Paues's printed text on pp. 227–237 only. It omits her introductory study on pp. 217–226 and does not reproduce McClean 123 outside fols. 115r–120r. Within that selected text the XML preserves all 337 L elements, Paues's footnotes and marginal comparison notes, printed page milestones, manuscript folio milestones, local line figures, and italic Trinity-comparison line figures.

The two-line Sinners Beware fragment and 335-line Poema Morale version are conceptually separate content parts but not separate leaves or XML divisions: they run continuously from f. 115r within a single LG. Paues considered the first couplet either a copying mistake from an exemplar containing both works or a deliberately suitable motto; DIMEV calls it a verse-couplet heading. The record preserves that uncertainty.

extent.description: Two conceptually distinct verse components represented continuously in one XML LG: 2 lines from the opening of Sinners Beware plus the 335-line McClean Poema Morale version. The 337-line total describes CME00070 and this witness selection, not the abstract Poema Morale, which DIMEV describes as 398 lines in couplets in its fullest form.

content_parts.label: Opening couplet from Sinners Beware
content_parts.summary: A two-line invocation asking the Holy Ghost for guidance and protection. Paues identifies it as the opening of Sinners Beware and discusses its possible placement as either scribal error or motto.

locators.value: f. 115r, first two copied lines immediately before Poema Morale

notes: The fragment is physically continuous with the following Poema Morale text; this part boundary is analytical, not a claim of a separate leaf, rubric, or XML division.
DIMEV indexes the complete Sinners Beware separately as NIMEV 3607 / record-5698 and describes it as fifty-nine six-line stanzas; CME00070 contains only these two opening lines.

content_parts.label: Poema Morale: McClean 123 version
content_parts.summary: The 335-line M version of the anonymous late-twelfth-century moral verse homily, beginning 'Ich am elder þane ich was of wintre & of lore' and ending with a prayer to reach Christ after death.

locators.value: pp. 227–237, beginning after the two-line opening couplet
ff. 115r–120r, beginning immediately after the two-line opening couplet

notes: Paues's italic figures at the right align selected M lines with Trinity witness T; they do not make T the base text.
DIMEV's 398-line extent describes the abstract work in its fullest witnesses, not this shorter 335-line version.

related_works.title: Poema Morale
notes: The composite unit contains the complete shorter McClean version, not all 398 lines found in the fullest Poema Morale witnesses.

related_works.title: Sinners Beware
notes: Only the first two lines are present. Paues reports uncertainty over whether they were copied accidentally from a shared exemplar or deliberately used as a motto.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME00070.xml.
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem']/LG; L[1–337]; PB 227–237; folio and line MILESTONE elements; final review TEMPHEAD
evidence.summary: Establishes the exact 337-line encoded selection, physical and printed locators, first and last lines, one-container structure, editorial notes, italics, and U-M identifier.

evidence.citation: Element, attribute, and content audit of repository CME00070.xml.
evidence.locator: Direct counts and ordered L-text comparison under ETS/EEBO/TEXT/BODY/DIV1
evidence.summary: Counts one DIV1, one LG, 337 L, 36 NOTE, eleven PB, 227 MILESTONE and 38 italic HI spans. The first two L elements match Sinners Beware and the remaining 335 form the McClean Poema Morale version. The XML folio marker 155b is an encoding error for 115b confirmed against Paues's page 228.

evidence.citation: Anna C. Paues, 'A Newly Discovered Manuscript of the Poema Morale,' Anglia 30, new series 18 (1907): 217–237.
evidence.locator: complete article PDF pp. 1–21; introduction pp. 1–10 / printed 217–226; edited text pp. 11–21 / printed 227–237
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for Paues's direct McClean transcription, the ff. 115r–120r locus, the two opening Sinners Beware lines and their uncertain function, the 335-line Poema Morale text, witness comparison, transcription conventions, and exact edition boundary.

evidence.citation: Betty Hill, 'The Twelfth-Century Conduct of Life, Formerly the Poema Morale or A Moral Ode,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 9 (1977): 97–144.
evidence.locator: complete article; witness list pp. 97–99; date and provenance pp. 109–115; metre and homiletic discussion pp. 115–144
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly evidence for title history, the seven complete versions, M and T loci, Paues's 1905 transcription, late-twelfth-century date arguments, tentative South-East Midlands localization, metre, genre and religious-didactic content.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, Poema Morale record-2113 / NIMEV 1272 and Sinners Beware record-5698 / NIMEV 3607.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-2113, wit-2113-3 and wit-2113-4; record-5698; data/Manuscripts.xml CamFitzMc123 and CamTCC335; data/Bibliography.xml Paues1907
evidence.summary: Versioned scholarly index evidence for identifiers, preferred title, Poema incipit and 398-line couplet extent, moral-advice subject, M and T folio loci, the two-line heading, the separate Sinners Beware record and Paues edition.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, A Descriptive Catalogue of the McClean Collection of Manuscripts in the Fitzwilliam Museum (Cambridge, 1912), no. 123.
evidence.locator: printed pp. 262–267; Internet Archive PDF pages 305–310 / scan leaves 304–309
evidence.summary: Published Fitzwilliam catalogue evidence for McClean 123's shelfmark, codex extent, support, dimensions, binding, contents and Nuneaton provenance inscriptions.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00070: Biblissima+ McClean 123 date statement.
evidence.summary: Binds the descriptive 1275–1325 normalization to Biblissima's attributed manuscript date while preserving Paues's and James's broader late-thirteenth-century or circa-1300 descriptions.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, The James Catalogue of Western Manuscripts, B.14.52.
evidence.locator: Poema Morale begins f. 2r and ends f. 9v; next sermon begins f. 10r; current codex date and physical description
evidence.summary: Official current evidence for the comparison witness's shelfmark, thirteenth-century catalog date, 91-folio extent, Poema locus and complete IIIF manifest.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00070: McClean 123 public-facsimile search and complete-object request route.
evidence.summary: Binds the descriptive record to the dated negative for a public item-specific McClean 123 facsimile, metadata-only records, Fitzwilliam onsite and reproduction routes, complete-object request specification, rights evidence, and exact source URLs.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00070: audited complete Trinity B.14.52 facsimile.
evidence.summary: Binds the comparison-witness statement to the complete 216-canvas local bundle, exact download manifest, audited inventory and Poema mapping at canvases 13–28 / ff. 2r–9v.

open_questions.question: Can the original Poema Morale be dated or localized more securely than late-twelfth-century composition and Hill's tentative southern South-East Midlands reconstruction around Middlesex or London?
next_steps: Review scholarship after Hill on the earliest T witness, metre, rhyme and dialect layers.
Keep composition claims separate from the language and production places of individual copies.

open_questions.question: Were the two Sinners Beware lines before the McClean Poema Morale copied accidentally from an exemplar containing both works, or intentionally used as a suitable motto or heading?
next_steps: Inspect a complete high-resolution facsimile of McClean 123 for mise-en-page, ruling, initials, spacing, punctuation and any rubric around the transition on f. 115r.
Compare the couplet with the complete Sinners Beware witness and later textual scholarship.

open_questions.question: How should the McClean copy's witness language be localized given Paues's Kentish-with-Western-admixture description, Hill's discussion of South-Eastern or Essex features, and DIMEV's LAEME-derived central Gloucestershire grid reference?
next_steps: Consult the live LAEME corpus entry and later linguistic scholarship for McClean 123.
Do not transfer any witness-language localization to the abstract work's composition place without explicit textual argument.

open_questions.question: Can a complete color digital facsimile of McClean 123 be obtained and what exact permissions would govern private research, public Git redistribution and commercial print-on-demand use?
next_steps: Ask the Fitzwilliam Image Library for a whole-object quotation covering covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves and endleaves, every manuscript leaf including blanks and adjacent works, and edges where offered.
Identify ff. 115r–120r separately as the target portion and request an image-inventory-to-folio crosswalk.
Request separate written terms for private research storage, public repository redistribution, and commercial or print-on-demand reuse before publishing supplied images.

notes: Paues's italic letters represent expanded abbreviations or supplied text, while italic figures at the right map M to Trinity witness T; neither convention should be interpreted as modern semantic emphasis.
The repository XML milestone 155b is a transcription or encoding error: Paues's printed page 228 labels the corresponding manuscript folio 115b, modernized here as f. 115v.

The fifteenth-century Nuneaton ownership inscriptions explain the names Nuneaton Book and Nuneaton Codex but do not prove that the manuscript, Poema Morale, or Sinners Beware was composed or copied at Nuneaton.

The complete Trinity B.14.52 facsimile is a comparison source only. The direct physical source for the Paues/CME text remains McClean 123, for which no complete public item-specific image object was found as of the review date.
Any newly supplied McClean images should be cached as the complete obtainable manuscript object and mapped to ff. 115r–120r, rather than downloading or preserving only the work portion.
